us-politics.headline: US-Iran Conflict Escalates as Military Deployments and Strikes Intensify
us-politics.body:
The United States and Iran are locked in a deepening military confrontation, with Iran striking Gulf refineries and the Trump administration responding with threats to attack Iranian infrastructure including bridges and power plants. B-52 bombers have been deployed to the region, and the USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier is returning to the Middle East, signaling a significant escalation in American military posture.

Iran's strategic position remains formidable, particularly its ability to threaten the Strait of Hormuz, through which a substantial share of global oil supplies pass. Analysts note this gives Tehran considerable leverage over the United States and global energy markets, complicating Washington's military calculus. The evacuation of U.S. troops from a Mideast base has prompted community support efforts stateside.

Domestically, the conflict is generating political headwinds for the Trump administration. A Washington Post report indicates the public was not persuaded by Trump's prime-time push for the Iran war, with rising gas prices adding to voter unease. Families of slain service members have been cited by Trump as urging him to "finish the job," framing the conflict as a matter of national honor and obligation.

sources.title: Iran hits Gulf refineries as Trump warns U.S. will attack Iranian bridges, power plants
Iran ‘toll booth’ on Strait of Hormuz underscores its leverage on Trump, US
Trump pushed the Iran war in prime time. The public wasn’t sold.
What B-52 bombers bring to Iran fight — and what it means for the war now
WATCH: President Trump reveals families of slain US service members urged him ‘finish the job’
Evacuation of U.S. troops from Mideast base sends community groups scrambling to help
USS Gerald R. Ford aircraft carrier to return to Middle East amid Iran fight

us-politics.headline: DOJ in Turmoil: Bondi Ousted, Epstein Inquiry and Voter Data Concerns Raised
us-politics.body:
Attorney General Pam Bondi has been removed from her position, triggering a significant shake-up at the Department of Justice. Reporting suggests her failure to deliver high-profile prosecutions — described as Trump's desired "scalps" — was central to her dismissal. The Hill identifies five major unresolved questions hanging over the DOJ following her departure, including the direction of ongoing investigations and leadership continuity.

Bondi's ouster has cast fresh uncertainty over the Epstein inquiry. Representative Ro Khanna (D-CA) has publicly questioned the future of the investigation, and NPR reports that despite the release of Epstein files, very few arrests have followed, raising questions about prosecutorial will and DOJ priorities under shifting leadership.

Separately, a key DOJ privacy officer has resigned ahead of a planned transfer of state voter data to the Department of Homeland Security, a move that has drawn sharp criticism from civil liberties advocates. Critics argue the data-sharing arrangement raises serious privacy concerns, while the administration frames it as a necessary step toward election integrity. The confluence of these developments has intensified scrutiny of the DOJ's independence and direction.

sources.title: Rep. Ro Khanna, D-Calif., on the future of the Epstein inquiry with Bondi's ousting
Five questions hanging over the DOJ shake-up
The Memo: Bondi’s failure to get Trump his ‘scalps’ sealed her fate
After the release of the Epstein files, why have there been so few arrests?
As DOJ prepares to share state voter data with DHS, a key privacy officer resigns

us-politics.headline: Trump Administration Faces Scrutiny Over Civil Liberties, Campus Policies, and Endorsements
us-politics.body:
The Trump administration is facing criticism on multiple civil liberties fronts. Advocacy groups have raised alarm over a White House demand that the University of Pennsylvania provide a list of Jewish faculty as part of an antisemitism probe, with critics calling the request "incredibly chilling" and reminiscent of discriminatory government overreach. The administration contends the inquiry is aimed at combating antisemitism on campus.

On other domestic policy fronts, Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has signed a memo opening the door for troops to carry personal firearms on military bases, a move welcomed by Second Amendment advocates but questioned by some military policy experts. Republicans in Congress are also pushing to defund NIH research they characterize as transgender-related animal testing, framing it as fiscal responsibility.

In electoral politics, Trump's re-endorsement of Republican Congressman Jeff Hurd — after briefly withdrawing support — highlights the unpredictable nature of the president's political alliances. Meanwhile, Palantir's immigration-related government contracts are putting Democratic donors who have ties to the company in an uncomfortable position, and a political outsider, Graham Platner, is gaining traction among Maine Democrats ahead of a Senate race.

sources.title: ‘Incredibly chilling’: Advocacy groups raise alarm over Trump’s demand for list of Jewish faculty at Penn
Palantir-linked campaign donations put Democrats in tight spot
Graham Platner’s political outsider vibe gains steam in Maine among Democrats
How Trump came to re-endorse a Republican congressman he had just ditched
GOP targets transgender animal testing in defund demand to NIH
Pete Hegseth signs memo opening door for troops to carry personal firearms on bases

us-politics.headline: 2028 GOP Race Takes Shape Amid Party Chaos and Vance's Diplomatic Moves
us-politics.body:
The Republican Party's internal dynamics are already shaping a nascent 2028 presidential contest, with Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio emerging as the two most prominent potential contenders. Analysis from The Hill frames the rivalry through the lens of the broader turbulence of the Trump era, suggesting that the chaotic political environment could benefit or undermine either candidate depending on how events unfold.

Vance is meanwhile conducting active foreign policy, with a planned visit to Hungary to meet Prime Minister Viktor Orbán just days before Hungary's parliamentary elections. The trip has drawn attention given Orbán's close ideological alignment with the American populist right. Supporters see the meeting as pragmatic diplomacy; critics argue it signals an endorsement of Orbán's democratic backsliding.

On the communications front, Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt has been defending the administration's SAVE America Act at public events, while also sharing details about her informal appointment by Trump. Separately, Trump's imposition of tariffs of up to 100% on some brand-name drug imports has been framed by the White House as an "America First" healthcare policy, though it has drawn pushback from industry figures, including a celebrity chef involved in America 250 planning.

sources.title: The Vance versus Rubio 2028 nomination fight through the lens of chaos
VP Vance to meet with Viktor Orbán in Hungary days ahead of foreign nation's elections
Celebrity chef lashes out at Trump for changing the 'rules' the same year as America 250

Trump slaps up to 100% tariff on some brand-name drug imports in major America First push

au-politics.headline: Australia's Fuel Security Crisis Deepens Amid Industry and Government Concerns
au-politics.body:
Australia is facing a significant fuel security challenge, with industry representatives warning that current fuel reserves heading to the country would not last a month. The shortage is being felt across the Asia-Pacific region, with reports highlighting the day-to-day impact on ordinary workers and businesses throughout Asia.

Politicians have put forward several proposals to address the crisis, though analysts and critics have questioned the viability of many of these solutions, describing some ideas as falling short of meaningful reform. The situation has prompted debate about Australia's long-term energy resilience and its dependence on imported fuel supplies.

The crisis also intersects with broader global energy debates. Commentators have noted a tension between nations committed to net-zero emissions targets and their continued reliance on oil, raising questions about the pace and practicality of the energy transition. Energy Minister Chris Bowen has been under pressure to respond, with the industry calling for more substantive and urgent government action to secure supply chains.

sources.title: Fuel heading to Australia won't last a month, industry says
A day in the life of Asia’s fuel crisis
‘Not a fix’: the three questionable ideas politicians are pitching to solve Australia’s fuel crisis
Net Zero nations trapped in mad race for oil

au-politics.headline: Easter Long Weekend Brings Tragedy, Tradition and Community Across Australia
au-politics.body:
Australians marked Good Friday with a mix of cultural traditions and community gatherings, including seafood meals, church services, and local events such as a boat race. Sydney's newly opened Fish Market drew large crowds during the holiday rush, reflecting the enduring tradition of seafood consumption on Good Friday.

However, the long weekend was also marked by tragedy on Australian roads. Three people were killed in separate crashes in Western Australia, including a 14-year-old dirt bike rider. A motorcyclist also died in a crash on the Monaro Highway in southern New South Wales, and another was killed in a traffic light collision in Adelaide, with a second rider hospitalised. Authorities have urged motorists to exercise caution over the remainder of the holiday period.

On the sporting front, the Easter weekend featured significant AFL and NRL action. Fremantle claimed a dramatic thriller after an Adelaide Crow hit the post in the final minute, while Penrith put in a dazzling performance against Melbourne Storm, who conceded 50 points for the first time since 2003. The long weekend also brought calm weather across most of the country, though the Bureau of Meteorology was monitoring a potential cyclone.

sources.title: Dirt bike rider, 14, among three dead in separate WA Easter long weekend crashes
Highway closed after motorcyclist dies in crash in southern NSW
Motorcyclist killed in traffic light crash in Adelaide, another rushed to hospital

au-politics.headline: Trump Administration Shake-Up and Tariffs Draw International Attention and Concern
au-politics.body:
The Trump administration has continued to make headlines internationally, with a series of high-profile firings drawing scrutiny. Among those removed were several prominent female allies of the President, including Attorney-General Pam Bondi, prompting analysis about the shifting dynamics within the administration. Commentators across the political spectrum have noted the breadth and pace of the personnel changes.

Australia has been directly affected by one of Trump's latest trade moves, with the administration announcing a 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into the United States. The Australian government has responded by stating it will not raise domestic drug prices as a result, seeking to reassure the public that the PBS and medicine affordability will be protected. The move has nonetheless raised concerns about the broader implications for trade relations.

Separately, a former US envoy involved in the Iran nuclear deal has strongly criticised a recent Trump speech on Iran, describing it as "delusional." This comes as Myanmar's junta chief Min Aung Hlaing was formally elected president, a development that has drawn international concern about democratic backsliding in the region.

sources.title: Trump's fiercest female defenders are now on the outer
Donald Trump is going on a firing spree
Australia says it won’t raise drug prices after Trump’s 100% tariff on pharmaceuticals imported into US
'Every word is delusional': Trump's speech lashed by former US envoy to Iran
Myanmar junta chief Min Aung Hlaing elected president

au-politics.headline: Australian Safety and Security: Child Abuse Sentencing and Teen Terror Charges
au-politics.body:
A Queensland man from North Lakes has been sentenced to 15 years in prison after being convicted of child abuse offences that included live-streaming the abuse of a child. The case has drawn significant attention to the issue of online child exploitation and the role of digital platforms in facilitating such crimes. Authorities and advocates have used the sentencing to renew calls for stronger protections for children online.

In a separate security matter, two New South Wales teenagers have been charged in connection with violent extremist material allegedly linked to terrorism. Police allege the material was of a nature that raised serious concerns about radicalisation among young people. The charges have prompted discussion about how extremist content spreads online and the challenges law enforcement faces in identifying and intervening early.

Together, these cases have highlighted ongoing concerns about the safety of vulnerable Australians, particularly children and young people, in both physical and digital environments. Authorities have emphasised their commitment to pursuing such cases vigorously, while community groups have called for greater investment in prevention and education programs.

sources.title: Queensland man jailed for 15 years after live-streaming child abuse
Two NSW teenagers charged over violent extremist material allegedly linked to terror

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates as Gulf States Face Missile and Drone Strikes
world.body:
Now in its 35th day, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran has expanded dramatically, with Tehran striking regional infrastructure in retaliation. Kuwait suffered significant damage after missile and drone attacks hit a major desalination plant and an oil refinery, raising fears of a broader regional conflagration drawing Gulf states into the conflict.

Iran has vowed further retaliation following a deadly US airstrike on a bridge in Karaj, with Tehran condemning what it calls a US-Israeli 'moral collapse' over attacks on civilian sites. Iran-allied paramilitaries in Iraq are also reportedly escalating their involvement, raising concerns that Baghdad could be dragged deeper into the war.

The conflict is sending shockwaves through global energy markets. China's independent 'teapot' refineries are helping Beijing weather the oil supply disruption, while Pakistan has seen an LNG surplus rapidly transform into a looming shortage. The crisis is testing regional alliances and supply chains across Asia and the Middle East, with no clear diplomatic off-ramp yet in sight.

sources.title: Kuwait desalination plant, oil refinery hit by missile and drone strikes
Iran condemns US-Israeli ‘moral collapse’ after attacks on civilian sites

Iran vows retaliation after deadly US strike on bridge in Karaj

world.headline: Trump Fires Attorney General Bondi and Army Chief Amid Washington Shake-Up
world.body:
President Donald Trump has dismissed Attorney General Pam Bondi, replacing her with Todd Blanche in a move that has stunned Washington observers. Bondi, once considered a close Trump loyalist, was removed without a detailed public explanation. Blanche, who previously served as Trump's personal defense attorney, will now lead the Justice Department, raising fresh questions about the independence of federal law enforcement.

The shake-up did not stop there. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth also fired the US Army Chief of Staff as part of a reported broader string of military dismissals, continuing a pattern of purges within the Pentagon's senior leadership. Critics have warned that the removal of experienced military commanders could undermine institutional stability.

Separately, Trump announced a sweeping 100 percent tariff on pharmaceutical imports, framing it as leverage to force foreign drug manufacturers into domestic production deals. The move adds to an already aggressive trade posture and is expected to draw strong reactions from major pharmaceutical exporters in Europe and Asia.

sources.title: Why did Trump fire Pam Bondi from Justice Department, who is Todd Blanche?
Hegseth fires US Army chief of staff in reported string of dismissals

Trump unveils 100 percent tariff on drugs to push for pharmaceutical deals

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Leaves Earth's Orbit, Heading Toward the Far Side of the Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II mission has reached a historic milestone, with its crew successfully leaving Earth's orbit and firing engines to set course for the far side of the Moon. The mission marks the first time humans have traveled beyond low Earth orbit in over five decades, representing a major step in NASA's broader goal of returning astronauts to the lunar surface.

The spacecraft's engine burn was confirmed as nominal, placing the crew on a precise trajectory for a lunar flyby. The mission is designed to test the Orion capsule and Space Launch System under deep-space conditions with a human crew aboard, paving the way for the subsequent Artemis III crewed lunar landing.

The four-person crew is expected to loop around the Moon before returning to Earth, a journey of roughly ten days. The mission has drawn global attention as a symbol of renewed ambitions in human space exploration, with international partners closely watching its progress.

sources.title: Artemis II leaves Earth's orbit on track for far side of the Moon
Artemis II mission fires up engines in next step of moon mission
Artemis II astronauts leave Earth's orbit, rocket towards Moon

world.headline: Myanmar's Coup Leader Becomes President; Panama Papers Turn Ten; Global Briefs
world.body:
Myanmar's military coup leader, General Min Aung Hlaing, has been formally elected president by the country's pro-military parliament, consolidating his grip on power more than five years after the 2021 coup that plunged the nation into a brutal civil war. The move is widely seen as an attempt to legitimise military rule ahead of a planned but widely disputed election.

This week also marks ten years since the Panama Papers leak, one of the largest financial data breaches in history. While the revelations exposed widespread offshore tax avoidance and corruption among world leaders and elites, analysts note that systemic change has been limited, with many jurisdictions still offering significant financial secrecy.

In other news, a baby was shot dead in a pram in New York City in a shocking incident that has prompted renewed calls for gun control measures. In India, a scorching early summer is raising fears that the cost of water and beer could soar due to severe strain on water resources. Tiger Woods also made headlines after it emerged he told police following a car crash that he had been speaking with the US president at the time of the incident.

sources.title: Ten years since Panama Papers: What did they reveal, did anything change?
Tiger Woods told police after car crash he was ‘talking to the president’

ai.headline: OpenAI Acquires Media Property TBPN, Raising Editorial Independence Concerns
ai.body:
OpenAI has acquired TBPN, a founder-focused business talk show that had built a reputation in tech circles for its candid coverage of the startup ecosystem. The deal marks an unusual move for an AI company into media ownership, blurring the line between subject and storyteller.

Critics and observers have been quick to note the conflict of interest inherent in a major AI company owning a outlet that covers the AI industry. Wired's framing was pointed — the acquisition effectively 'buys itself some positive news coverage,' raising questions about editorial integrity going forward.

The purchase reflects a broader trend of tech giants seeking to shape their own narratives as scrutiny of AI companies intensifies. Whether TBPN will maintain its independent voice or become a de facto promotional vehicle for OpenAI remains to be seen, but the acquisition has already generated significant skepticism across the tech press.

sources.title: OpenAI Acquires Tech Talk Show ‘TBPN’—and Buys Itself Some Positive News
OpenAI acquires TBPN, the buzzy founder-led business talk show
OpenAI just bought TBPN
OpenAI Acquires TBPN

ai.headline: AI Privacy Under Fire: Perplexity, Granola, and Proton Face Scrutiny
ai.body:
A wave of privacy concerns is hitting AI and tech products this week. Perplexity AI is facing a lawsuit alleging that its 'Incognito Mode' is a 'sham,' with plaintiffs claiming the feature does not actually prevent user data from being tracked or retained — a serious allegation for a product that markets itself as a trustworthy search alternative.

Separately, AI note-taking app Granola has drawn attention after it emerged that notes are publicly viewable by anyone with a link by default. The Verge flagged the issue as a significant privacy risk, particularly given that Granola also uses note content for AI training purposes, compounding concerns about user data exposure.

Meanwhile, a critical analysis of Proton Meet found that the privacy-focused video conferencing tool may not live up to the strong guarantees Proton has implied in its marketing. Together, these stories paint a picture of an industry where privacy claims frequently outpace reality, and users are left to discover the gaps themselves.

sources.title: Perplexity's "Incognito Mode" is a "sham," lawsuit says
PSA: Anyone with a link can view your Granola notes by default
Proton Meet Isn't What They Told You It Was

ai.headline: Europe Pushes Back on US Tech Dominance Amid Regulatory and Sovereignty Battles
ai.body:
European digital sovereignty is under pressure on multiple fronts. The EU has drawn sharp criticism from digital rights advocates after reportedly yielding to US pressure on key digital regulations, with critics calling it a 'fatal decision' that could undermine years of effort to establish independent tech governance standards in Europe.

At the same time, grassroots and institutional efforts to build European alternatives are gaining momentum. A new resource cataloguing European substitutes for over 120 US applications — from Google to Dropbox — has surfaced, reflecting growing demand for homegrown options. Switzerland, meanwhile, is positioning itself as a hub for semiconductor research, hosting what some are calling the 'CERN of semiconductor research,' a major initiative aimed at reducing European dependence on US and Asian chip supply chains.

On the healthcare front, NHS staff in the UK are reportedly refusing to use the Federated Data Platform due to ethical concerns about its operator, Palantir, highlighting how distrust of US tech firms is influencing adoption of digital tools even within critical public services.

sources.title: 'Fatal decision': EU slammed for caving to US pressure on digital rules
Show HN: European alternatives to Google, Apple, Dropbox and 120 US apps
Switzerland hosts 'CERN of semiconductor research'
NHS staff refusing to use FDP over Palantir ethical concerns

ai.headline: New Rowhammer GPU Exploit and Google's AI Video Push Headline Tech Developments
ai.body:
A newly disclosed Rowhammer attack variant has raised serious security alarms, with researchers demonstrating that the technique can give attackers complete control over machines running Nvidia GPUs. The attack exploits physical memory vulnerabilities and is particularly concerning given how widely Nvidia hardware is deployed in AI data centers and cloud infrastructure, where a compromise could have cascading consequences.

On the AI product side, Google has upgraded its Vids platform with its Veo video generation and Lyria music models, and introduced directable AI avatars — a significant step toward fully AI-generated video content for business and creative use. The update signals Google's intent to compete aggressively in the generative media space.

Elsewhere, the AI coding assistant Cursor released version 3, and a new tool called Apfel is offering Mac users a free, locally-run AI assistant. Google also confirmed funding for a new data center that will be powered by a large natural gas plant, reigniting debate about the energy footprint of AI infrastructure at a time when renewables accounted for 86 percent of newly installed global generating capacity in 2025.

sources.title: New Rowhammer attacks give complete control of machines running Nvidia GPUs

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Darkens as Bear Market Signals Mount Ahead of Holiday Weekend
btc.body:
Bitcoin is flashing increasingly bearish signals as multiple market indicators converge on a cautious outlook. On-chain data shows that the share of Bitcoin supply held in profit has dropped to levels analysts associate with a 'true bear market,' suggesting significant unrealized losses across the holder base. Futures markets are also tilting bearish, with volatility cooling and price action consolidating rather than recovering.

Adding to the pressure, Bitcoin is heading into a holiday weekend with reduced institutional liquidity, as both ETF flows and CME futures trading go offline. This leaves the market more exposed to sharp moves with fewer stabilizing forces in play. Miner behavior reinforces the cautious mood — Riot Platforms sold 3,778 BTC during Q1, reflecting a broader strategic shift among miners responding to sustained market pressure.

Together, these signals paint a picture of a market searching for a floor rather than building toward a recovery, with thin weekend liquidity raising the stakes for any unexpected macro or geopolitical developments.

sources.title: Crypto consolidates as volatility cools and futures markets tilt bearish
Bitcoin supply in profit heads to ‘true bear market’ levels
Bitcoin heads into holiday weekend exposed as ETF and CME flows go offline
Bitcoin miner Riot sold 3,778 BTC during Q1 amid broader market pressure

btc.headline: DeFi Security Under Scrutiny as Hackers Steal $169M and Drift Pursues Exploiter
btc.body:
Decentralized finance continued to grapple with serious security vulnerabilities in the first quarter of 2026, with hackers stealing $169 million across 34 DeFi protocols, according to data from DefiLlama. While the figure represents a decline compared to prior periods, it underscores that exploits remain a persistent and costly threat to the ecosystem.

In a notable development, Drift Protocol took the unusual step of sending an on-chain message directly to wallets linked to a $280 million exploit, attempting to open a dialogue with the attacker. The tactic, increasingly used in high-profile hacks, reflects the limited enforcement options available to DeFi teams and their growing reliance on public pressure and blockchain transparency to recover funds.

The string of incidents highlights ongoing challenges around smart contract security and the difficulty of protecting user funds in permissionless environments, even as the industry matures and security auditing practices improve.

sources.title: Drift sends onchain message to wallets tied to $280M exploit
Crypto hackers steal $169M from 34 DeFi protocols in Q1: DefiLlama

btc.headline: Stablecoins, Tokenization, and Wrapped Bitcoin Signal Maturing Crypto Infrastructure
btc.body:
A cluster of developments this week points to crypto infrastructure quietly reaching new levels of institutional relevance. Stablecoins surpassed automated clearing house (ACH) transaction volume in February, a milestone that signals their growing role as a practical payments rail rather than a speculative asset. The figure underscores how digital dollars are beginning to compete directly with legacy financial plumbing.

On the tokenization front, the IMF acknowledged that tokenizing financial assets improves efficiency and settlement but warned it also introduces new risks, including liquidity mismatches and potential systemic vulnerabilities. Meanwhile, Circle announced plans to launch cirBTC, a wrapped Bitcoin product targeting institutional markets and positioning itself as a competitor to existing offerings from BitGo and Coinbase.

Rounding out the infrastructure story, South Korean brokerage Korea Investment & Securities is reportedly eyeing a stake in crypto exchange Coinone, reflecting continued traditional finance interest in gaining direct crypto market exposure. Collectively, these moves suggest the boundary between conventional finance and digital assets is eroding steadily.

sources.title: South Korean brokerage Korea Investment & Securities eyes Coinone stake: Report
Tokenization makes finance more efficient but introduces risks: IMF
Stablecoins flip automated clearing house volume in February
Circle to launch cirBTC wrapped Bitcoin, challenging BitGo and Coinbase

btc.headline: Quantum Threats, AI Payments, and a New Crypto-Friendly Interim AG Shape Policy Landscape
btc.body:
The longer-term technological and regulatory backdrop for crypto shifted on several fronts. Naoris Protocol launched what it describes as a quantum-resistant blockchain, directly addressing the so-called 'Q-Day' threat — the point at which sufficiently powerful quantum computers could break the cryptographic foundations underpinning Bitcoin and Ethereum. The launch signals that quantum preparedness is moving from theoretical concern to active development.

On the AI and payments frontier, a coalition of major technology firms backed the new x402 Foundation, an initiative designed to advance agentic AI adoption through standardized payment protocols. The project builds on crypto payment rails to enable autonomous AI agents to transact, pointing to a convergence of AI and blockchain infrastructure.

In Washington, Todd Blanche — the author of the DOJ's crypto enforcement memo that reshaped how the department approaches digital asset cases — has been named interim Attorney General. His elevation is likely to be closely watched by the industry, given his direct role in crafting enforcement policy that many in crypto viewed as a significant shift toward a more measured regulatory posture.

sources.title: Naoris Protocol's quantum-resistant blockchain goes live as Bitcoin and Ethereum face 'Q-Day' threats
Big Tech firms back new x402 Foundation to advance agentic AI adoption
Todd Blanche, author of DOJ crypto enforcement memo, is now interim AG

food.name: Manti (Turkish Lamb Dumplings with Yogurt & Chilli Butter)
food.description: Tiny hand-pinched dumplings filled with spiced minced lamb, drowned in garlicky yogurt and a sizzling paprika-butter sauce — a dish where the contrast of hot and cold is the whole point.
food.tip: Make the dumplings as small as you can manage; tradition holds that a proper manti should fit ten in a spoon, and the smaller they are the better the sauce-to-filling ratio.

exercise.name: Friday Kettlebell Complex
exercise.equipment: One kettlebell (12–24 kg depending on level)
exercise.description: Warm up with 5 minutes of hip circles, halos, and goblet squats. Then perform 5 rounds of a 5-move complex without putting the bell down: 5 deadlifts, 5 Romanian deadlifts, 5 swings, 5 cleans, 5 press — each side. Rest 90 seconds between rounds. Finish with 5 minutes of slow Turkish get-up practice on each side. Hits the whole body and leaves a satisfying end-of-week fatigue.

learn.topic: The Antikythera Mechanism — the world's first analogue computer
learn.description: Pulled from a Roman-era shipwreck off the Greek island of Antikythera in 1901, this corroded bronze device from around 100 BCE turns out to be a hand-cranked orrery that predicted solar and lunar eclipses, tracked the Metonic calendar cycle, and modelled the irregular motion of the Moon using a pin-and-slot epicyclic gear train — a mechanical concept not seen again in Europe for over a thousand years. Recent X-ray tomography and the 2021 UCL reconstruction revealed it likely also displayed all five planets known to antiquity.
learn.why: It fundamentally rewrites assumptions about ancient Greek technological sophistication, and the ongoing effort to fully decode it — researchers still debate several gear functions — is a live, genuinely open puzzle sitting at the intersection of archaeology, mechanical engineering, and the history of science.
